summary: Italian modernist architect, Pritzker Prize winner. Designed the Centre Pompidou in Paris (with Richard Rogers) — famous for its inside-out, color-coded exterior pipes and tubes. Also designed The Shard (London's tallest building), Kansai International Airport (on an artificial island in Osaka Bay), the Whitney Museum of American Art (New York), the Menil Collection (Houston), the Modern Wing of the Art Institute of Chicago, and the Jean-Marie Tjibaou Cultural Centre in Noumea, New Caledonia.

works.name: Centre Pompidou (1977)
works.description: Co-designed with Richard Rogers (and engineer Peter Rice). A cultural complex in Paris featuring a modern art museum and library. Famous for its 'inside-out' design — elevators, electrical wiring, and plumbing are color-coded and displayed on the outside of the building. Named for former French President Georges Pompidou. Wikipedia

examples: collaborated with Richard Rogers on a complex in Paris with a distinctive inside-out look
a museum that has its elevators, electrical wiring, and plumbing in colored tubes on the outside
worked closely with Richard Rogers on a building noted for its use of multi-colored tubing

clues.clue: Named for a former President of France (Georges Pompidou)
examples: With an American, he designed a color-coded building complex named for a former President of France.
It was named for a former French president and includes color-coded pipes.

clues.clue: Piano was the alphabetically-first designer (before Rogers)
examples: This man was the alphabetically-first designer of a museum that has its elevators, electrical wiring, and plumbing in colored tubes on the outside.

clues.clue: Contains a modern art museum and a library
examples: Piano and Richard Rogers designed this Paris building that includes a modern art museum and a library.

clues.clue: Rogers's Lloyd's Building is a related high-tech building across from the Willis Building in London
examples: After separating from Foster, Rogers collaborated with this architect on a building whose exterior is covered with color-coded tubes.

works.name: The Shard (2012)
works.description: A glass-clad pyramidal skyscraper in London — the tallest building in the United Kingdom and (at time of completion) the EU. Built on the site of the demolished Southwark Towers. Inspired by Canaletto's paintings of London's spires. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Tallest building in the United Kingdom (and formerly the EU); a glass-clad pyramidal skyscraper
examples: A glass-clad pyramidal skyscraper designed by this architect is the tallest building in the United Kingdom.
the tallest building in the European Union

clues.clue: Inspired by Canaletto's paintings of London's spires; the steep pyramidal shape was chosen for this reason
examples: Scenes by Canaletto depicting the spires of London inspired the steep pyramidal shape that this architect used in his design for the tallest building in the EU.

clues.clue: Built on the site of the demolished Southwark Towers
examples: The Southwark Towers were demolished to make room for one of this man's designs, the tallest building in the European Union.

works.name: Kansai International Airport (1994)
works.description: An airport terminal in Osaka Bay, Japan, built on an artificial island (Kankujima). Features the world's longest airport terminal — a mile-long departure lounge. Wing-shaped; its sliding joints helped the terminal survive the 1995 Kobe earthquake unscathed. The building was inspired by the shape of an airfoil. Collaborator: Noriaki Okabe. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Built on an artificial island (Kankujima) in Osaka Bay, Japan
examples: For 10 points, name this Italian architect of Osaka's Kansai International Airport and The Shard.
an airport inspired by the shape of an airfoil on an artificial island in Osaka Bay, the Kansai International Airport

clues.clue: Features the world's longest airport terminal — a mile-long departure lounge
examples: A mile-long departure lounge is part of the world's longest airport terminal, which this architect designed on the artificial island of Kankujima.
This man's use of sliding joints helped a wing-shaped building survive the 1995 Kobe earthquake unscathed despite its mile-long departure lounge.

clues.clue: Sliding joints helped the wing-shaped building survive the 1995 Kobe earthquake unscathed
examples: This man's use of sliding joints helped a wing-shaped building survive the 1995 Kobe earthquake unscathed despite its mile-long departure lounge.

clues.clue: Designed with collaborator Noriaki Okabe
examples: This designer of the new Whitney Museum and the Shard collaborated with Noriaki Okabe on the longest airport terminal in the world.

works.name: Whitney Museum of American Art (2015)
works.description: A new building for the Whitney Museum in New York City's Meatpacking District, near the High Line. Features a multi-level system of terraces and a cantilevered entrance leading to a 'largo.' Replaced a Marcel Breuer-designed building. Wikipedia

He included a multi-level system of terraces in his design for a museum at the southern entrance of the High Line park.

works.name: Jean-Marie Tjibaou Cultural Centre (1998)
works.description: A cultural center in Noumea, New Caledonia, built to celebrate Kanak culture. Features ten pavilions (called 'cases') inspired by traditional Kanak Great Houses. Uses specially designed louvers called 'nacos' on the exterior to regulate wind flow. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Uses specially designed louvers called 'nacos' on the exterior to regulate wind flow

works.name: Menil Collection (1987)
works.description: A museum in Houston, Texas. Features ferroconcrete 'leaves' on the roof that serve as both a heat source and protection against UV light, maximizing natural light. Contains a Cy Twombly Pavilion. Wikipedia

This architect utilized ferroconcrete 'leaves' on the roof of the Menil Collection in Houston, Texas.

works.name: Modern Wing, Art Institute of Chicago (2009)
works.description: An addition to the Art Institute of Chicago. Like many of Piano's museums, natural light is maximized in the design. Connected to Millennium Park by the Nichols Bridgeway. Wikipedia

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Renzo Piano is an Italian architect, winner of the Pritzker Prize. He is principally associated with Richard Rogers as his collaborator on the Centre Pompidou. His designs consistently maximize natural light in museums. He converted an automobile manufacturing plant into a convention center and trade fair district.

clues.clue: Italian architect, Pritzker Prize winner
examples: Name this Pritzker Prize-winning architect who co-designed the Pompidou Centre with Richard Rogers.

clues.clue: Consistent use of natural light in museum designs (a trademark)
examples: Like many of his other museums, the use of natural light is maximized in this architect's design for the modern wing of the Art Institute of Chicago.

clues.clue: Converted a gigantic automobile manufacturing plant into a convention center and trade fair district
examples: He converted a gigantic automobile manufacturing plant into a convention center and trade fair district.

comprehensive_summary:
Renzo Piano is an Italian architect and Pritzker Prize winner most famous in quizbowl for two works: the Centre Pompidou in Paris and The Shard in London. The Centre Pompidou was co-designed with Richard Rogers (Piano being the alphabetically-first designer) and is celebrated for its 'inside-out' aesthetic — elevators, electrical wiring, and plumbing are color-coded and displayed on the building's exterior, with external escalators. The complex contains a modern art museum and a library and was named for former French President Georges Pompidou. Rogers later went on to design the Lloyd's Building in London, while Piano designed The Shard — a glass-clad pyramidal skyscraper inspired by Canaletto's paintings of London's spires — which became the tallest building in the United Kingdom, built on the site of the demolished Southwark Towers.

Piano also designed Kansai International Airport in Osaka, Japan, built on the artificial island of Kankujima in Osaka Bay. It features the world's longest airport terminal — a mile-long departure lounge — and a wing-shaped design whose sliding joints allowed it to survive the 1995 Kobe earthquake unscathed. His collaborator on this project was Noriaki Okabe. In New York, Piano designed the new Whitney Museum of American Art in the Meatpacking District, replacing a Marcel Breuer-designed building with a terraced structure opening onto the High Line, featuring a cantilevered entrance leading to a 'largo.'

In New Caledonia, Piano designed the Jean-Marie Tjibaou Cultural Centre in Noumea to celebrate Kanak culture — ten pavilions ('cases') inspired by traditional Kanak Great Houses, with specially designed louvers called 'nacos' on the exterior to regulate wind flow. His Menil Collection museum in Houston features ferroconcrete 'leaves' on the roof that serve as both a heat source and UV protection, maximizing natural light — a consistent Piano trademark. This museum also contains a Cy Twombly Pavilion. The Modern Wing of the Art Institute of Chicago similarly maximizes natural light and is connected to Millennium Park by the Nichols Bridgeway, which Piano also designed.

Among Piano's other works: the Zentrum Paul Klee in Bern (wavy steel beams blending into surrounding hills); the California Academy of Sciences (green roof inspired by San Francisco's seven hills); a renovation of Harvard's Fogg Museum that preserved the brick facade while adding a glass pyramid roof; an expansion of the Morgan Library with three glass-and-steel pavilions; the Parliament House of Malta (Gozitan limestone, zero-emission design); the Kimbell Art Museum expansion in Fort Worth; the Biosfera glass-and-steel structure in Genoa's Porto Antico (housing 150 flora and fauna species); the Padre Pio Pilgrimage Church; the NEMO Science Museum in Amsterdam; Aurora Place in Sydney; and a redesign of Valletta City Gate in Malta, where he preserved the ruins of Edward Middleton Barry's Royal Opera House as an open-air space.
